name: Linear Scan with Overflow Guard
summary: Walk the string once: skip spaces, read an optional sign, then fold digits into a running total — checking BEFORE each digit whether the next multiply would overflow, and clamping if so.
visualizationIntent: init: show the string; the cursor sits at index 0. move: the cursor walks past leading spaces, which dim out. check: the sign character is marked special. update: each digit is consumed, lighting visited, and folded into the running total res. check: before folding, test whether res would exceed the 32-bit limit; if so clamp and stop. return: show the final signed, clamped integer.

lineExplanations.3: Record the string's length so the cursor knows where to stop.
lineExplanations.4: Start the cursor at the first character.
lineExplanations.5: While the cursor is on a space, keep skipping.
lineExplanations.6: Step the cursor one character to the right.
lineExplanations.7: Remember where the real content starts — everything before it was whitespace.
lineExplanations.8: Assume the number is positive unless we see a minus.
lineExplanations.9: No sign character has been seen yet (-1 = none).
lineExplanations.10: Is the first real character a minus sign?
lineExplanations.11: Yes — the result will be negative.
lineExplanations.12: Remember which character was the sign.
lineExplanations.14: Otherwise, is it a plus sign?
lineExplanations.15: Remember which character was the sign.
lineExplanations.17: The running total starts at zero.
lineExplanations.20: While the cursor is on a digit, keep converting.
lineExplanations.21: Turn the digit character into its numeric value 0-9.
lineExplanations.22: Would folding this digit in push res past the 32-bit maximum?
lineExplanations.23: If the number is positive, clamp to the maximum.
lineExplanations.27: Return immediately — a clamped value is final.
lineExplanations.28: Shift res left one digit and add the new one.
lineExplanations.29: Step the cursor to the next character.
lineExplanations.30: Apply the sign to the accumulated magnitude.

syntaxExplanations.1: class Solution: is the class wrapper LeetCode expects.
syntaxExplanations.2: def myAtoi(self, s): s is the input string to parse.
syntaxExplanations.3: len(s) is the number of characters in s.
syntaxExplanations.4: pos = 0 puts the cursor at the first character.
syntaxExplanations.5: s[pos] == ' ' checks for a space; 'and' short-circuits so we never read past the end.
syntaxExplanations.7: scan_start = pos saves where non-space content begins.
syntaxExplanations.9: sign_idx = -1 records that no sign has been seen (-1 is not a valid index).
syntaxExplanations.10: s[pos] == '-' tests for a minus sign.
syntaxExplanations.12: sign_idx = pos remembers the sign's position.
syntaxExplanations.13: pos = pos + 1 steps past the sign.
syntaxExplanations.14: elif runs only if the minus test failed; s[pos] == '+' tests for plus.
syntaxExplanations.15: sign_idx = pos remembers the sign's position.
syntaxExplanations.16: pos = pos + 1 steps past the sign.
syntaxExplanations.20: s[pos] >= '0' and s[pos] <= '9' is a digit test using character order.
syntaxExplanations.21: ord(c) - ord('0') converts a digit character to its value 0-9.
syntaxExplanations.22: // is integer division; comparing against int_max // 10 detects overflow before it happens.
syntaxExplanations.23: if sign == 1: chooses which bound to clamp to.
syntaxExplanations.24: res = int_max pins the answer at the maximum.
syntaxExplanations.26: res = int_min pins the answer at the minimum.
syntaxExplanations.27: return res exits early with the clamped value.
syntaxExplanations.28: res * 10 + digit appends the digit as the new ones place.
syntaxExplanations.29: pos = pos + 1 advances to the next character.
syntaxExplanations.30: sign * res applies the sign to the magnitude.
syntaxExplanations.31: return res hands back the final integer.
